rulings.Case Title: BUMBLE, INC. ET AL VS. CONOR GRAY
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 5, 2025, line 1, PETITIONERS BUMBLE, INC., BUMBLE TRADING LLC, BUMBLE HOLDING LIMITED'S Motion To Confirm Arbitration Award Petitioners Bumble Inc.; Bumble Trading LLC; and Bumble Holding Limited seek to confirm the arbitration award entered on October 17, 2024, and seek a final judgment based on the award. A party may seek a court judgment confirming an arbitration or appraisal award by filing and serving a petition at least 10 days, but no more than four years after the award is served. (CCP, 1288 and 1288.4) The court must confirm the award as made, unless it corrects or vacates the award, or dismisses the proceeding. (Id. 1286.) If the award is confirmed, an enforceable judgment is entered with the same force and effect as a judgment in a civil action. (Id., 1287.4.) Petitioners attach the award as Exhibit E to the Vierra Declaration. No proof of service showing when the award was served is attached, but because Petitioners served the Vierra Declaration on Respondent Conor Gray by overnight delivery and mail on January 7, 2025, and the date of hearing is more than 10 days after the award, the court finds compliance with the procedural requirements recited above. The unopposed motion is granted and the award is confirmed. The award demonstrates that Petitioners are entitled to costs they incurred after the offer to compromise was rejected. (Vierra Dec., Ex. C, at p. 4.) Petitioners are ordered to prepare a final judgment stating substantially as follows: The motion of Bumble Inc.; Bumble Trading LLC; and Bumble Holding Limited, Petitioners, for an order confirming the arbitration award rendered herein on October 17, 2024, came on regularly for hearing before the undersigned on February 4, 2025, upon notice duly and regularly given. Good cause appearing therefor, IT IS ORDERED that the award of the arbitrator dated October 17, 2024, be confirmed. Petitioners are entitled to costs of suit herein. Petitioners ask for costs to be awarded with the judgment. The court denies the request without prejudice. Petitioners may seek these costs as set out in Rule of Court 3.1700. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: THE GAP, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION VS. SVES GO, LLC, A FLORIDA LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: CROSS COMPLAINANT SVES GO, LLC'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE THE ORDER FOR WRIT OF ATTACHMENT PURSUANT TO CCP 485.240 (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 5, 2025, line 5, CROSS COMPLAINANT SVES GO, LLC'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE THE ORDER FOR WRIT OF ATTACHMENT PURSUANT TO CCP 485.240 (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2) Economic Duress It is unlikely that defendant will be able to establish the narrow defense of economic duress. A claim of economic duress requires (1) a wrongful demand/act that (2) leaves the party with no reasonable alternative but to succumb. (Rich & Whillock, Inc. v. Ashton Development, Inc. (1984) 157 Cal.App.3d 1154, 1158-1159.) "It is contended that the taking of legal action or the threat to take such action cannot constitute duress. This is generally true." (Leeper v. Beltrami (1959) 53 Cal. 2d 195, 204.) While false or frivolous litigation could amount to duress, Plaintiff's lawsuit is not frivolous. The court further concludes that defendant/cross-complainant is unlikely to prevail on its claims. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. rulings.Case Title: THE GAP, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION VS. SVES GO, LLC, A FLORIDA LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside The Order For Writ Of Attachment Pursuant To Ccp 485.240; Memorandum Of Point And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 5, 2025, line 5, CROSS COMPLAINANT SVES GO, LLC'S Motion To Set Aside The Order For Writ Of Attachment Pursuant To Ccp 485.240 (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Defendant SVES GO, LLC's motion to set aside the order for writ of attachment (entered on August 14, 2024) per CCP section 485.240 is denied. "[A]lthough in a motion under section 485.240 the defendant is the moving party, the plaintiff nevertheless continues to have the burden of proving (1) that his claim is one upon which an attachment may be issued and (2) the probable validity of such claim, the same burden he must meet under section 484.090." (Loeb & Loeb v. Beverly Glen Music, Inc. (1985) 166 Cal.App.3d 1110, 1116). Here, the parties dispute whether plaintiff's claim for breach of contract has probable validity. (CCP section 481.190 ["A claim has 'probable validity' where it is more likely than not that the plaintiff will obtain a judgment against the defendant on that claim."] If the defendant opposes the application, "the court must consider the relative merits of the positions of the respective parties and make a determination of the probable outcome of the litigation." (Loeb & Loeb, 166 Cal. App.3d at 1120.) The weight of the evidence shows that plaintiff's claim for breach of contract has probable validity and defendant is unlikely to establish its defenses or claims. Plaintiff seeks to recover on the "Settlement Agreement and Third Amendment to Reseller Agreement" ("Third Amendment"). (Barker Decl., Ex. C.) The Cornwell declaration demonstrates that: (1) the parties executed the Third Amendment on October 3, 2023; (2) defendant promised to pay $26,176,000.00 in installments; (3) defendant defaulted on the Third Amendment and only made partial payments; and (4) plaintiff was damaged. The court also finds compelling-but not dispositive-the recitals in the Third Amendment where the parties agreed that plaintiff was not in default while defendant was. In opposition, the Murciano declarations are conclusory and lacking factual support. In any event, plaintiff sufficiently rebuts their viability. Fraud Defendant argues that plaintiff provided non-conforming goods and "[u]pon information and belief, GAP knew but intentionally failed to disclose to SVES that they never intended to ship the regular-sized goods ordered by SVES." (Murciano Decl., par. 8.) The Barker declaration shows that defendant was unable to identify what was already in its warehouses. (Barker Decl., par. 10.) He further notes that the parties had a similar dispute when they entered into the "Second Amendment to Authorized Reseller Agreement," where defendant agreed to give notice of product discrepancies within 45 days. (Barker Decl., pars. 8-12, 15.) Defendant therefore agreed that 45-days was a reasonable time to report discrepancies. It is reasonable to infer that shoddy record-keeping and defendant's failure to comply with its 45-day obligation caused much of the confusion. Defendant had months to inspect the product prior to agreeing to the Third Amendment. (Barker Decl., par. 29.) Defendant claims that an unnamed Gap representative assured defendant that plaintiff fulfilled its obligations and would not file a public lawsuit against defendant. Again, the declaration is lacking. Lack of Consideration This defense is dubious. "A written instrument is presumptive evidence of a consideration." (Civil Code section 1614.) Plaintiff gave defendant approximately a $5,000,000 discount to effectuate the Third Amendment. The amount of consideration necessary in any case is merely that of a peppercorn. (Third Story Music, Inc. v. Waits (1995) 41 Cal.App.4th 798, 808, fn. 5.) (end of tentative ruling part 1 see part 2) = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: A2 ACADEMY LLC ET AL VS. SWORD HEALTH, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, February 5, 2025, line 6, 1 - DEFENDANTS SWORD HEALTH, INC., SWORD HEALTH, S.A., VIRGILIO BENTO'S MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT Defendants' motion to strike is DENIED as moot. The demurrer to the first cause of action has been sustained. Thus, the motion to strike Paragraph 46 of the FAC is moot because that paragraph falls within the first cause of action to which a demurrer has been sustained. Plaintiffs shall file and serve an amended complaint, if any, or notify Defendants they are abandoning the breach of contract cause of action, within 20 days of notice of entry of this order.
